paper_title: Finite Kripke models and provability interpretations in quantified modal logic

perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation removes the requirement for 'universal closures' of the K-axiom. In quantified modal logic, axioms must be universally closed to ensure that the logic remains closed under Generalization when formulas contain free variables.

perturbations.original:
\begin{definition}[Kripke frames]
A \emph{Kripke frame} for quantified modal logic is a triple $\mathcal{F} = (W,R,\{D_w\}_{w \in W})$, where
\begin{itemize}
    \item $W$ is a nonempty set,
    \item $R$ is a binary relation on $W$,
    \item for each $w \in W$, $D_w$ is a nonempty set,
    \item if $x R y$, then $D_x \subseteq D_y$. 
\end{itemize}
We say a Kripke frame $\mathcal{F}$ is \emph{finite} if $W$ and all $D_w$ are finite sets. 
A Kripke frame $\mathcal{F}$ is said to be \emph{constant domain} if $D_x = D_y$ for all $x, y \in W$. 
When a Kripke frame is constant domain, we write $\mathcal{F} = (W,R,D)$.
\end{definition}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{definition}[Kripke frames]
A \emph{Kripke frame} for quantified modal logic is a triple $\mathcal{F} = (W,R,\{D_w\}_{w \in W})$, where
\begin{itemize}
    \item $W$ is a nonempty set,
    \item $R$ is a binary relation on $W$,
    \item for each $w \in W$, $D_w$ is a nonempty set,
    \item if $x R y$, then $D_y \subseteq D_x$. 
\end{itemize}
We say a Kripke frame $\mathcal{F}$ is \emph{finite} if $W$ and all $D_w$ are finite sets. 
A Kripke frame $\mathcal{F}$ is said to be \emph{constant domain} if $D_x = D_y$ for all $x, y \in W$. 
When a Kripke frame is constant domain, we write $\mathcal{F} = (W,R,D)$.
\end{definition}
perturbations.why_wrong: The monotonicity condition for Kripke frames is reversed ($D_y \subseteq D_x$ instead of $D_x \subseteq D_y$). Standard Kripke semantics for quantified modal logic requires expanding domains (or at least non-decreasing) to ensure that individuals existing in a world continue to exist in accessible worlds.

perturbations.original:
\begin{definition}[Kripke models]
A \emph{Kripke model} is a tuple $\mathcal{M} = (W, R, \{D_w\}_{w \in W}, \Vdash)$, where $\Vdash$ is a relation between elements of $W$ and atomic formulas with parameters from the corresponding domains, extended inductively as follows:
For each $w \in W$, 
\begin{itemize}
    \item $w \nVdash \bot$ and $w \Vdash \top$,
    \item $w \Vdash \neg A \iff w \nVdash A$,
    \item $w \Vdash A \land B \iff w \Vdash A \text{ and } w \Vdash B$,
    \item $w \Vdash A \lor B \iff w \Vdash A \text{ or } w \Vdash B$,
    \item $w \Vdash A \to B \iff w \nVdash A \text{ or } w \Vdash B$,
    \item $w \Vdash \forall x A \iff \forall a \in D_w \, (w \Vdash A(a))$,
    \item $w \Vdash \exists x A \iff \exists a \in D_w \, (w \Vdash A(a))$,
    \item $w \Vdash \Box A \iff \forall v \in W\,(w R v \Rightarrow v \Vdash A)$.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{definition}[Kripke models]
A \emph{Kripke model} is a tuple $\mathcal{M} = (W, R, \{D_w\}_{w \in W}, \Vdash)$, where $\Vdash$ is a relation between elements of $W$ and atomic formulas with parameters from the corresponding domains, extended inductively as follows:
For each $w \in W$, 
\begin{itemize}
    \item $w \nVdash \bot$ and $w \Vdash \top$,
    \item $w \Vdash \neg A \iff w \nVdash A$,
    \item $w \Vdash A \land B \iff w \Vdash A \text{ and } w \Vdash B$,
    \item $w \Vdash A \lor B \iff w \Vdash A \text{ or } w \Vdash B$,
    \item $w \Vdash A \to B \iff w \nVdash A \text{ or } w \Vdash B$,
    \item $w \Vdash \forall x A \iff \forall v \in W \, (w R v \Rightarrow \forall a \in D_v \, (v \Vdash A(a)))$,
    \item $w \Vdash \exists x A \iff \exists a \in D_w \, (w \Vdash A(a))$,
    \item $w \Vdash \Box A \iff \forall v \in W\,(w R v \Rightarrow v \Vdash A)$.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}
perturbations.why_wrong: The truth condition for the universal quantifier $\forall x A$ is incorrectly defined by quantifying over domains of accessible worlds ($D_v$) rather than the current world's domain ($D_w$). This conflates the meaning of the quantifier with the modal operator.

The perturbation claims $\QK$ is complete with respect to constant domain frames. However, constant domain frames validate the Barcan Formula ($\QKbf$), which is not a theorem of $\QK$. $\QK$ is only complete with respect to the class of all Kripke frames (with varying domains).

The paper explicitly defines QKbf as the logic obtained by adding the Barcan formula to QK. Constant domain models require the Barcan formula to be valid; therefore, the logic QK (without the Barcan formula) cannot be complete with respect to constant domain frames.

The theorem specifically restricts the equivalence to sentences in which only one variable appears. Removing this restriction makes the claim false, as quantified modal logic is generally not complete with respect to finite frames for all sentences.

perturbations.original:
\begin{theorem}[Formalized L\"ob's theorem]
Suppose that $\PR_T(x)$ satisfies $\DC{2}$ and $\DC{3}$.
Then, for any $\LA$-sentence $\varphi$, $\PA \vdash \PR_T(\gn{\PR_T(\gn{\varphi}) \to \varphi}) \to \PR_T(\gn{\varphi})$.
\end{theorem}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{theorem}[Formalized L\"ob's theorem]
Suppose that $\PR_T(x)$ satisfies $\DC{1}$ and $\DC{2}$.
Then, for any $\LA$-sentence $\varphi$, $\PA \vdash \PR_T(\gn{\PR_T(\gn{\varphi}) \to \varphi}) \to \PR_T(\gn{\varphi})$.
\end{theorem}
perturbations.why_wrong: Löb's theorem requires the third derivability condition (DC3), which is the formalized version of the principle that if something is provable, it is provably provable. DC1 (the rule of necessitation) is a meta-level property, whereas the formalized theorem requires the internal property DC3.

perturbations.original:
\begin{definition}[Uniform derivability conditions]
\leavevmode
\begin{description}
    \item [$\DCU{1}$]
    if $T \vdash \forall \ora{x}\,\varphi(\ora{x})$, then $\PA \vdash \forall \ora{x}\,\PR_T(\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})})$.
    
    \item [$\DCU{2}$] $\PA \vdash \forall \ora{x}\,
    \bigl(
    \PR_T(\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})\to \psi(\ora{\dot{x}})})
    \to
    (\PR_T(\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})}) \to \PR_T(\gn{\psi(\ora{\dot{x}})}))
    \bigr)$. 

    \item [$\DCU{3}$] $\PA \vdash \forall \ora{x}\,
    \bigl(
    \PR_T(\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})})
    \to
    \PR_T(\gn{\PR_T(\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})})})
    \bigr)$.
    
    \item [$\SCU$]
    if $\varphi(\ora{x})$ is a $\Sigma_1$-formula, then $\PA \vdash \forall \ora{x}\,
    \bigl(
    \varphi(\ora{x}) \to \PR_T(\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})})
    \bigr)$.
\end{description}
Here, $\ora{x}$ is a finite tuple of variables. 
So, $\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})}$ is an abbreviation for $\gn{\varphi(\dot{x}_0, \ldots, \dot{x}_{m-1})}$ that is a primitive recursive term corresponding to a primitive recursive function calculating the G\"odel number of $\gn{\varphi(\num{n_0}, \ldots, \num{n_{m-1}})}$ from $(n_0, \ldots, n_{m-1})$. 
\end{definition}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{definition}[Uniform derivability conditions]
\leavevmode
\begin{description}
    \item [$\DCU{1}$]
    if $T \vdash \forall \ora{x}\,\varphi(\ora{x})$, then $\PA \vdash \forall \ora{x}\,\PR_T(\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})})$.
    
    \item [$\DCU{2}$] $\PA \vdash \forall \ora{x}\,
    \bigl(
    \PR_T(\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})\to \psi(\ora{\dot{x}})})
    \to
    (\PR_T(\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})}) \to \PR_T(\gn{\psi(\ora{\dot{x}})}))
    \bigr)$. 

    \item [$\DCU{3}$] $\PA \vdash \forall \ora{x}\,
    \bigl(
    \PR_T(\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})})
    \to
    \PR_T(\gn{\PR_T(\gn{\varphi(\ora{x})})})
    \bigr)$.
    
    \item [$\SCU$]
    if $\varphi(\ora{x})$ is a $\Sigma_1$-formula, then $\PA \vdash \forall \ora{x}\,
    \bigl(
    \varphi(\ora{x}) \to \PR_T(\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})})
    \bigr)$.
\end{description}
Here, $\ora{x}$ is a finite tuple of variables. 
So, $\gn{\varphi(\ora{\dot{x}})}$ is an abbreviation for $\gn{\varphi(\dot{x}_0, \ldots, \dot{x}_{m-1})}$ that is a primitive recursive term corresponding to a primitive recursive function calculating the G\"odel number of $\gn{\varphi(\num{n_0}, \ldots, \num{n_{m-1}})}$ from $(n_0, \ldots, n_{m-1})$. 
\end{definition}
perturbations.why_wrong: In the condition $\DCU{3}$, the inner G\"odel number uses $\ora{x}$ instead of the dotted variables $\ora{\dot{x}}$. This breaks the uniform property because the G\"odel number must be calculated from the values of the variables (numerals) to satisfy the condition for all $x$ inside the provability predicate.

The paper states that DCU1 and DCU2 imply SCU. Replacing DCU2 with DCU3 is incorrect because DCU2 (the distribution of provability over implication with parameters) is essential for the proof of Sigma-1 completeness (SCU).

The paper notes that Fefermanian predicates satisfy DCG2 (a specific version of the distribution axiom). Replacing DCG2 with DCU3 is incorrect because, as the context of S0015 explains, DCU3 is not redundant for Fefermanian predicates because they are not necessarily Sigma-1.

perturbations.original:
\begin{definition}[Arithmetical interpretations]
Let $\PR_T(x)$ be a provability predicate of $T$.
An \emph{arithmetical interpretation} based on $\PR_T(x)$ is a map $f$ from modal formulas to $\LA$-formulas having the same free variables satisfying the following conditions: 
\begin{itemize}
    \item if $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate symbol and $f(P(x_0,\ldots,x_{n-1})) \equiv \varphi(x_0, \ldots, x_{n-1})$, then $f(P(y_0,\ldots, y_{n-1})) \equiv \varphi(y_0, \ldots, y_{n-1})$, 
    \item $f(A \circ B) \equiv f(A)\circ f(B)$ for $\circ \in \{\land,\lor,\to\}$,
    \item $f(\neg A) \equiv \neg f(A)$,
    \item $f(Q x A) \equiv Q x\, f(A)$ for $Q \in \{\forall, \exists\}$,
    \item $f(\Box A(x_0, \ldots, x_{n-1})) \equiv \PR_T(\gn{f(A)(\dot{x}_0, \ldots, \dot{x}_{n-1})})$.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{definition}[Arithmetical interpretations]
Let $\PR_T(x)$ be a provability predicate of $T$.
An \emph{arithmetical interpretation} based on $\PR_T(x)$ is a map $f$ from modal formulas to $\LA$-formulas having the same free variables satisfying the following conditions: 
\begin{itemize}
    \item if $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate symbol and $f(P(x_0,\ldots,x_{n-1})) \equiv \varphi(x_0, \ldots, x_{n-1})$, then $f(P(y_0,\ldots, y_{n-1})) \equiv \varphi(y_0, \ldots, y_{n-1})$, 
    \item $f(A \circ B) \equiv f(A)\circ f(B)$ for $\circ \in \{\land,\lor,\to\}$,
    \item $f(\neg A) \equiv \neg f(A)$,
    \item $f(Q x A) \equiv Q x\, f(A)$ for $Q \in \{\forall, \exists\}$,
    \item $f(\Box A(x_0, \ldots, x_{n-1})) \equiv \PR_T(\gn{f(A)(x_0, \ldots, x_{n-1})})$.
\end{itemize}
\end{definition}
perturbations.why_wrong: The definition of $f(\Box A)$ is incorrect because it uses the variables $x_i$ directly inside the G\"odel brackets $\gn{\cdot}$ instead of the dotted variables $\dot{x}_i$. Arithmetical interpretations must use the num function (represented by dots) to map the values of free variables to their corresponding numerals in the provability predicate.

The perturbation changes Sigma-1 to Delta-1. The context states that the formula weakly represents T in PA. According to the definitions in the paper, weak representation of a recursively enumerable theory like T (which is PA or an extension) requires a Sigma-1 formula; a Delta-1 formula would imply the theory is decidable, which contradicts the nature of T in this context.

The theorem specifically claims the existence of a Sigma-2 formula. Changing this to Delta-1 is incorrect because the construction of the predicate for this specific result (as indicated in the context of Lemma 4.10) relies on the higher complexity of Sigma-2 predicates to handle the height of the frame.

perturbations.original:
\begin{corollary}
    If a modal sentence $A$ is in 
    \[
        \bigcap \{\QPL(\PR_\tau) \mid \tau(v)\ \text{is a}\ \Sigma_2\ \text{formula weakly representing}\ T\ \text{in}\ \PA\},
    \]
    then $A$ is valid in all conversely well-founded finite Kripke frames. 
\end{corollary}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{corollary}
    If a modal sentence $A$ is in 
    \[
        \bigcap \{\QPL(\PR_\tau) \mid \tau(v)\ \text{is a}\ \Sigma_2\ \text{formula weakly representing}\ T\ \text{in}\ \PA\},
    \]
    then $A$ is valid in all finite Kripke frames. 
\end{corollary}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation removes the 'conversely well-founded' condition. The preceding theorem (Theorem 3.1) specifically constructs a counter-model using a 'conversely well-founded finite rooted Kripke model', and the proof outline for this corollary explicitly assumes the frame is conversely well-founded to handle the provability predicate's behavior.

The perturbation changes the existential quantifier in clause 1 to a universal quantifier. This is incompatible with clause 3; if for every k in Di, PA proved 'for all x, theta_k(x)', then for distinct k and l in Di, PA would prove 'exists x (theta_k(x) and theta_l(x))', directly contradicting clause 3.

perturbations.original:
\begin{corollary}
    If a modal sentence $A$ is in 
    \[
        \bigcap \{\QPL(\PR_T) \mid \PR_T(x)\ \text{is a}\ \Sigma_1\ \text{provability predicate of}\ T\ \text{satisfying}\ \DCG{2}\},
    \]
    then $A$ is valid in all finite constant domain Kripke frames. 
\end{corollary}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{corollary}
    If a modal sentence $A$ is in 
    \[
        \bigcap \{\QPL(\PR_T) \mid \PR_T(x)\ \text{is a}\ \Sigma_1\ \text{provability predicate of}\ T\ \text{satisfying}\ \DCG{1}\},
    \]
    then $A$ is valid in all finite constant domain Kripke frames. 
\end{corollary}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation changes the condition from D2 to D1. The context and the theorem it refers to (Theorem 3.3) explicitly require the provability predicate to satisfy the D2 modal axiom (distributivity of provability over implication) to ensure the logic behaves correctly in constant domain models.

perturbations.original:
\begin{corollary}
    Let $A$ be a modal sentence in which only one variable appears. 
    If $A$ is in
    \[
        \bigcap \{\QPL(\PR_T) \mid \PR_T(x)\ \text{is a}\ \Sigma_1\ \text{provability predicate of}\ T\ \text{satisfying}\ \DCG{2}\},
    \]
    then $\QKbf \vdash A$. 
\end{corollary}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{corollary}
    Let $A$ be a modal sentence in which only one variable appears. 
    If $A$ is in
    \[
        \bigcap \{\QPL(\PR_T) \mid \PR_T(x)\ \text{is a}\ \Sigma_1\ \text{provability predicate of}\ T\ \text{satisfying}\ \DCG{2}\},
    \]
    then $\mathsf{QK} \vdash A$. 
\end{corollary}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation replaces QKbf with QK. The paper discusses constant domain models, which are characterized by the Barcan Formula (bf). The text immediately following this corollary mentions 'rooted finite constant domain Kripke model', which corresponds to QKbf, not the weaker system QK.

The perturbation changes the universal quantifier in clause 2 to an existential quantifier. In the context of defining an arithmetical interpretation where theta_k(x) represents that x is the k-th element, the elements must be disjoint for all x (universal), not just for some x (existential), to ensure the interpretation is well-defined.
